narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your journey toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to passing the bar exam as a tough one—about a 72% chance of not making it through. Picture your story as seen through the model's lens: it started with your Year 3 grades landing you in the 2nd decile at school, which stood out as the biggest signal of struggle ahead, like a warning light that you've hit a wall in ramping up academically when it counts most. That low Year 1 decile of 2 echoed the same challenge early on, reinforcing a pattern of being in the lower ranks compared to peers. Your LSAT score of 25 then piled on, painting you as someone who might not have the raw test-taking edge needed for the bar's intensity. With a middle family income, the model worried about limited resources for extra tutoring or support that often tips the scales. Your Asian background and undergraduate GPA of 2.5 added layers, suggesting hurdles in a field where those markers sometimes predict extra pressure without the buffer to overcome them. Even being full-time and female nudged the outlook downward in this mix, as the model weighs how those fit into broader patterns of who pushes through. It's not that any one thing defines you—it's this combination that built a picture of high risk, like stacking hurdles too high for the bar's marathon. But here's the empowering part: this is just a snapshot based on patterns from others, and your determination could rewrite it—maybe by targeting those deciles or LSAT with focused prep. You've got the drive; now channel it to shift the story. We're rooting for your next chapter.
